modules.title: Introduction to Docker and Containers
modules.subtitle: Module 1: Understanding What Docker and Containers Are

slides.name: module1-title
slides.script: Welcome to Docker Containers for Beginners. In this module, we'll explore what Docker is, why containers matter, and how they revolutionize software deployment by making applications portable and consistent across environments.
slides.title: Introduction to Docker and Containers

slides.name: what-is-docker
slides.script: Docker is a platform that uses containerization technology to package applications with everything they need to run. Think of it like a shipping container for software—everything inside travels together, making deployment simple and reliable.
slides.title: What is Docker?
points: Docker packages software and dependencies into containers
Simplifies deployment on any system running Docker

slides.name: what-are-containers
slides.script: Containers are lightweight, standalone environments that run applications. Unlike virtual machines, they share the host OS kernel but keep apps isolated. This makes containers faster and less resource-heavy, ideal for modern development.
slides.title: What Are Containers?
points: Containers bundle app code and dependencies
Share OS kernel but remain isolated
Faster and lighter than virtual machines

slides.name: why-use-docker
slides.script: Using Docker simplifies development, testing, and deployment by ensuring your app behaves the same everywhere. It helps avoid the "works on my machine" problem, boosts productivity, and streamlines collaboration across teams.
slides.title: Why Use Docker?
points: Ensures consistent app behavior across environments

slides.name: docker-architecture
slides.script: Docker uses client-server architecture where the Docker client talks to the Docker daemon running on your machine. The daemon builds, runs, and manages containers. Images are read-only templates used to create containers.
slides.title: Docker Architecture Basics
points: Docker client sends commands to Docker daemon

slides.name: hello-world-example
slides.script: Let's run the classic hello-world container to see Docker in action. This command downloads the image if needed and runs the container, printing a friendly message. It proves your Docker setup works correctly.
slides.title: Running Your First Docker Container

slides.name: hello-world-explanation
slides.script: The command 'docker run hello-world' does three things. First, it checks if the image exists locally. If not, it downloads it. Next, it creates and starts the container. Finally, the container runs its code, printing the welcome message.
slides.title: What Just Happened?
points: Docker checked for the image locally
Started container and ran its program

slides.name: module1-summary
slides.script: In summary, Docker is a powerful tool that packages applications inside containers, ensuring consistency and portability. We covered what Docker and containers are, why they matter, and ran your first container. Next, we'll install Docker and set up your environment.
slides.title: Module 1 Summary
points: You ran your first Docker container

modules.title: Installing Docker and Basic Commands
modules.subtitle: Module 2: Setting Up Docker and Exploring Core Commands

slides.name: module2-title
slides.script: Now that we understand Docker basics, it's time to install Docker on your machine and learn some essential commands to manage containers and images effectively.
slides.title: Installing Docker and Basic Commands

slides.name: install-docker-overview
slides.script: Docker installation varies by operating system. On Windows and Mac, Docker Desktop provides an easy graphical installer. Linux users can install Docker Engine using package managers. We'll cover installation steps for each major OS.
slides.title: Installing Docker
points: Docker Desktop for Windows and Mac

slides.name: install-docker-windows
slides.script: For Windows, download Docker Desktop from the official Docker website. Run the installer, follow prompts, and enable WSL 2 backend if prompted. After installation, open PowerShell and type 'docker --version' to verify.
slides.title: Installing Docker on Windows
slides.code:
# Download and install Docker Desktop from docker.com
# Enable WSL 2 backend during setup
# Verify installation
docker --version

slides.name: install-docker-mac
slides.script: On Mac, download Docker Desktop dmg file from Docker's site. Open and drag Docker to Applications folder. Launch Docker, wait for it to start, then verify by running 'docker --version' in Terminal.
slides.title: Installing Docker on Mac
slides.code:
# Download Docker Desktop dmg
# Drag to Applications
# Launch and verify
docker --version

slides.name: install-docker-linux
slides.script: Linux installation uses package managers. For Ubuntu, run commands to update packages, install prerequisites, add Docker’s repo, then install Docker Engine. Finally, check installation with 'docker --version'.
slides.title: Installing Docker on Ubuntu Linux
slides.code:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install apt-transport-https ca-certificates curl software-properties-common
curl -fsSL https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/gpg | sudo apt-key add -
sudo add-apt-repository "deb [arch=amd64] https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu $(lsb_release -cs) stable"
sudo apt update
sudo apt install docker-ce

docker --version

slides.name: docker-basic-commands
slides.script: After installation, some basic Docker commands let you manage images and containers. 'docker pull' downloads images, 'docker run' starts containers, 'docker ps' lists running containers, and 'docker stop' stops them.
slides.title: Basic Docker Commands Overview

slides.name: pull-and-run-nginx
slides.script: Let's download the official Nginx web server image and run it. This command pulls the image if needed and starts a container exposing port 80 to your machine, letting you access a web server locally.
slides.title: Pulling and Running an Nginx Container

slides.name: expose-ports-explanation
slides.script: The '-d' flag runs the container in the background. The '-p 8080:80' maps port 8080 on your machine to port 80 inside the container, where Nginx listens. This setup lets you visit localhost:8080 in your browser to see the server.
slides.title: Explaining the Docker Run Flags
points: '-p 8080:80' maps host port to container port
Allows browser access to the container service

slides.name: list-containers
slides.script: To see running containers, use 'docker ps'. To list all containers, including stopped ones, use 'docker ps -a'. This helps track what containers are active or exist on your system.
slides.title: Listing Containers

slides.name: stop-remove-container
slides.script: You can stop a running container by its container ID or name using 'docker stop'. To remove a stopped container and free resources, use 'docker rm'. This keeps your environment clean and tidy.
slides.title: Stopping and Removing Containers

slides.name: module2-summary
slides.script: In this module, you installed Docker on your OS, learned how to pull and run containers, and explored core commands to manage them. These basics set the foundation for building and customizing your own Docker images in upcoming modules.
slides.title: Module 2 Summary
points: Used commands to list, stop, and remove containers

modules.title: Creating Docker Images with Dockerfiles
modules.subtitle: Module 3: Writing Dockerfiles to Build Custom Images

slides.name: module3-title
slides.script: Now that you can run containers, let's learn to create your own Docker images using Dockerfiles. A Dockerfile is a text file with step-by-step instructions for building an image tailored to your app.
slides.title: Creating Docker Images with Dockerfiles

slides.name: what-is-dockerfile
slides.script: A Dockerfile is like a recipe that tells Docker how to build an image. It defines a base image, copies files, installs dependencies, and sets commands to run inside the container. This automates image creation reproducibly.
slides.title: What Is a Dockerfile?
points: Text file listing steps to build an image
Specifies base image and app setup

slides.name: dockerfile-basic-example
slides.script: Here's a simple Dockerfile that creates an image running a Python app. It starts from the official Python base image, copies the app code, installs required packages, and defines the command to run the app.
slides.title: Simple Dockerfile Example
slides.code:
FROM python:3.9-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt ./
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
COPY . .
CMD ["python", "app.py"]

slides.name: dockerfile-explanation
slides.script: This Dockerfile starts with a lightweight Python base image. 'WORKDIR' sets the working directory inside the container. 'COPY' transfers files. 'RUN' installs dependencies. 'CMD' sets the default command to run your app when the container starts.
slides.title: Explaining the Dockerfile Instructions

slides.name: build-image-command
slides.script: To build an image from your Dockerfile, use 'docker build' with a tag name. The '.' tells Docker to use the current directory as context. This processes the Dockerfile steps and creates an image you can run anytime.
slides.title: Building the Docker Image

slides.name: run-custom-image
slides.script: Once built, run your custom image with 'docker run'. You can map ports or mount volumes if needed. This launches a container using your image, running the app as defined in the Dockerfile's CMD instruction.
slides.title: Running Your Custom Image

slides.name: dockerfile-best-practices
slides.script: Writing efficient Dockerfiles improves build speed and image size. Use official slim base images, minimize layers by combining commands, and avoid copying unnecessary files. This keeps your images lightweight and fast.
slides.title: Dockerfile Best Practices
points: Keep images lean for faster builds

slides.name: module3-summary
slides.script: This module showed how Dockerfiles let you create custom images by specifying build steps. You saw a sample Python app Dockerfile, learned how to build and run images, and discovered best practices to optimize your Dockerfiles.
slides.title: Module 3 Summary
points: Run containers from your custom images
Follow best practices for efficient images

modules.title: Docker Compose for Multi-Container Applications
modules.subtitle: Module 4: Orchestrating Containers with Docker Compose

slides.name: module4-title
slides.script: When building complex applications, you often need multiple containers working together. Docker Compose simplifies this by letting you define and run multi-container applications with a simple configuration file.
slides.title: Docker Compose for Multi-Container Applications

slides.name: what-is-docker-compose
slides.script: Docker Compose is a tool that uses a YAML file to configure your app's services, networks, and volumes. Instead of running containers one by one, you start them all with a single command, managing dependencies easily.
slides.title: What Is Docker Compose?
points: Defines multi-container apps in a YAML file
Starts all containers with one command

slides.name: simple-compose-example
slides.script: Here's a basic docker-compose.yml file for a web app using Nginx and Redis. It defines two services, their images, ports, and how they connect. This config file lets you spin up both containers together seamlessly.
slides.title: Basic Docker Compose YAML Example
slides.code:
version: '3'
services:
  web:
    image: nginx
    ports:
      - "8080:80"
  redis:
    image: redis

slides.name: compose-up-command
slides.script: To launch the services defined in your docker-compose.yml, run 'docker-compose up'. This command pulls images if missing, creates containers, and starts them, showing combined logs. Add '-d' to run in the background.
slides.title: Starting Services with Docker Compose

slides.name: compose-explanation
slides.script: Running 'docker-compose up' creates and starts all services, handling dependencies and networking automatically. It simplifies managing multiple containers as a single application, making development and deployment more efficient.
slides.title: How Docker Compose Works
points: Shows combined logs for easy monitoring

slides.name: compose-down-command
slides.script: When you're done, stop and remove all containers and networks created by Compose with 'docker-compose down'. This cleans up your environment, so nothing is left running or consuming resources.
slides.title: Stopping and Cleaning Up Compose

slides.name: volume-and-network-example
slides.script: You can extend your Compose file to define volumes for persistent data and custom networks for isolated communication. This example adds a volume for Redis data and a network shared by both services.
slides.title: Adding Volumes and Networks in Compose
slides.code:
version: '3'
services:
  web:
    image: nginx
    ports:
      - "8080:80"
    networks:
      - mynet
  redis:
    image: redis
    volumes:
      - redis-data:/data
    networks:
      - mynet
volumes:
  redis-data:
networks:
  mynet:

slides.name: module4-summary
slides.script: Docker Compose lets you easily define and run multi-container applications with a simple YAML file. We covered basic service definitions, starting and stopping apps, and adding volumes and networks for real-world app setups.
slides.title: Module 4 Summary
points: YAML file defines services and configs
One command starts or stops all containers

modules.title: Deploying Docker Containers
modules.subtitle: Module 5: Running Docker Containers in Production

slides.name: module5-title
slides.script: In this final module, we explore how to deploy Docker containers beyond your local machine, covering best practices for production-ready deployments and introducing container registries and orchestration basics.
slides.title: Deploying Docker Containers

slides.name: container-deployment-concepts
slides.script: Deploying containers means running them on servers accessible by users. Key considerations include image storage, orchestration, scaling, and monitoring. Production environments require stability, security, and automated workflows.
slides.title: Container Deployment Concepts
points: Containers run apps on production servers

slides.name: docker-hub-push
slides.script: Docker Hub is a popular public container registry. To share your image, first tag it with your Docker Hub username, then push it. This makes your image accessible anywhere Docker runs, enabling easy deployment.
slides.title: Pushing Images to Docker Hub
slides.code:
docker tag my-python-app yourusername/my-python-app:latest
docker push yourusername/my-python-app:latest

slides.name: image-pull-deploy
slides.script: On your server, you pull the image from Docker Hub using 'docker pull', then run it like before. This workflow separates building images from running containers, making deployment consistent and repeatable.
slides.title: Pulling and Running Images on Servers

slides.name: introduction-to-orchestration
slides.script: For deploying many containers, orchestration tools like Kubernetes or Docker Swarm manage container health, scaling, and updates. They automate complex tasks, ensuring high availability and easy management of containerized apps.
slides.title: Introduction to Container Orchestration

slides.name: deployment-best-practices
slides.script: Production deployments should use stable image tags, automated CI/CD pipelines, environment variable management, and secure networking. Monitoring and logging help maintain app health and quickly detect issues.
slides.title: Production Deployment Best Practices
points: Use versioned image tags, not latest
Automate builds and deployments with CI/CD

slides.name: module5-summary
slides.script: We’ve covered container deployment essentials: pushing images to registries, pulling and running on servers, and an intro to orchestration. Following best practices ensures your Docker apps run smoothly and reliably in production.
slides.title: Module 5 Summary
points: Push and pull images via registries
Follow best practices for stability and security
